name: グローバルラーニング（科学技術と社会技術） Ａ
summary_excerpt: 本授業は、「科学技術と社会技術」というテーマについて、多角的な視点(トピッ......
subject_name: グローバルラーニング（科学技術と社会技術） Ａ
subject_category: 基盤専門科目・グローバルラーニング
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
subtitle:
科学技術と社会技術
"Science and Technology" and "Social technology"
summary:
本授業は、「科学技術と社会技術」というテーマについて、多角的な視点(トピック)から話題提供と議論を行う内容となっている。また、この授業を通じて、「科学技術」と「社会技術」の関わりだけでも、様々な課題や視点があり、それらを取り巻く環境や諸課題も多岐に渡ることから、その課題解決に向けたアプローチも多くの学問領域が学際的かつ多角的であることが求められる。本授業における講義やワークショップ・ディスカッション等を含むアクティブラーニングを通じて、それらを体得する授業を展開する。なお、「科学技術と社会技術」をめぐり、ローカル・リージョナル・ナショナル・グローバルというそれぞれの視点が重要になり、本授業では地方自治体、国、EUや米国などの国際的な動向なども踏まえて新たな気づきを提供する。
This class will provide topics and discussions on "science and technology" and "social technology" from multiple perspectives. Through this class, students will also understand that there are various issues and perspectives regarding the relationship between "science and technology" and "society." Furthermore, because the environments and issues surrounding "science and technology" and "social technology" are diverse, approaches to solving these issues require many academic fields to be interdisciplinary and multifaceted. This class will provide students with the opportunity to acquire these knowledge through active learning, including lectures, workshops, and discussions. In addition, because the various perspectives of local, regional, national, and global are important when discussing "science and technology and social technology," this class will provide new insights based on local government, national, and international trends in the EU and the United States.
goal: ■本授業(「科学技術と社会技術」)に関連する様々なテーマについて主体的に問題意識をもち、その問題の本質とは何かを追究できる能力と知識を習得する。
study_outside:
【事前学習】各授業で予定しているトピックに関して紹介する文献等を読み、事前にどのような授業になるかイメージすること。各自プレゼン資料の作成が発生する場合がある。
Students will study the topics planned for each class in advance. They will also have an idea of what the class will be about. Students may need to prepare presentation materials.
【事後学習】各講義で取り扱った内容に関連する文献等を自ら探し理解を深める。
Students will be required to independently find literature and other materials related to the content covered in each lecture to deepen their understanding.

name: グローバルラーニング（地球社会と未来政策） Ａ
summary_excerpt: 本講義は、日本を含め国際社会で社会問題化している地球規模課題に対して、話題......
subject_name: グローバルラーニング（地球社会と未来政策） Ａ
subject_category: 基盤専門科目・グローバルラーニング
minor_field: 社会科学の今日的課題
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
subtitle:
地球社会と未来政策
Global Society and Future Policy
summary:
本講義は、日本を含め国際社会で社会問題化している地球規模課題に対して、話題提供等を行う。その上で、まず受講者個人がどのようなことを問題視しているのかを全体ディスカッション等を通じて共有や報告を行う。その後、自身の関心のある地球規模課題に対する現状や課題等を自ら調査し、課題解決に向けたアプローチ等を検討するプロセスで、最終的に関連する将来ビジョンやフューチャーデザイン等を描いた未来政策を実現するためのステートメントを発表する。各回のディスカッション内容等を踏まえて、本ステートメントをブラッシュアップして未来政策レポートとして提出することが求められる。本授業を通じて、主体的に問題(仮説)を設定し、日本や世界でどのようなことが議論されているか否かを含め現状を把握し、本質的な課題解決に向けて不確実な将来に対して今後どのような未来政策をデザインしていくべきかを検討する中で、大学生活で必要となる調査研究とその発表の基本的な一連のアプローチ方法を実践的に体得できるようになることが期待される。
This course will provide topics on several global issues and lecture on the basic ideas of future policy. Next, through discussion, you will share and report on what you think are the problems regarding global issues. Furthermore, you will independently research the current situation and issues regarding the global issues of your interest and consider approaches to solving the issues. Finally, you will present a statement for realizing future policies that outline related future visions and future designs. You will be required to refine this statement based on the content of each discussion, and submit a future policy report. Through this class, students will independently set problems, grasp the content and current situation of discussions around the world, and consider what kind of future policies will be required to be designed in order to solve essential issues. Through this process, it is expected that students will be able to practically acquire a series of basic approaches to research and presentation, which will be necessary in university life.
goal: ■本授業を通じて、自分の意見を分かりやすく論理的に表現・伝達するスキルを身に付け、同じ各テーマに対して他者がどのような見解をもっているか把握し議論することができる。

Prepare to present a future policy statement on a global issue that concerns you, and prepare to submit a final report.

name: 社会科学特講（リスクと不確実性の法政策学） Ａ
summary_excerpt: 本講義では、具体的に公害環境問題や先端科学技術の社会実装をめぐる諸問題を主......
subject_name: 社会科学特講（リスクと不確実性の法政策学） Ａ
subject_category: 先進専門科目・先進社会科学特殊講義
level: 中級レベル（発展・応用）
subtitle: リスクと不確実性の法政策学
summary: 本講義では、具体的に公害環境問題や先端科学技術の社会実装をめぐる諸問題を主な事例として、「リスク」と「不確実性」を明確に区別し、当該分野に対する法政策のあり方について多角的に検討をする。科学技術の発展に法整備が追い付いていない状況や具体的な公害環境問題に対する人的被害が生じてから法整備が検討されるなど、これら諸問題をめぐる「リスク」と「不確実性」に対する法政策は、日本を含め国際社会において喫緊の社会課題となっている。講義では、地方自治体、国、EUや米国などの国際的な動向なども踏まえて、関連する事例や課題解決に向けたアプローチの基本的な考え方を紹介し、適宜グループディスカッションや受講者の報告・発表などアクティブラーニングも取り入れて授業を展開する。
goal:
■本授業に関連する様々なテーマについて主体的に問題意識をもち、その問題の本質とは何かを追究できる能力と知識を習得する。
■本授業を通じて、「リスク」と「不確実性」にはどのような違いがあり、それらを取り巻く諸問題に対する法政策上の課題を理解できるようになる。
■「リスク」と「不確実性」をめぐる様々な社会問題にアンテナを張り、情報やデータを取捨選択・整理をして、自ら課題解決に向けた具体的なアプローチを考えることができるようになる。
study_outside:
【事前学習】各講義で予定しているトピックに関して、事前にどのような授業になるか予測・イメージしながら、関連情報や文献等を調べること。
【事後学習】各講義で取り扱った内容に関連する文献等を自ら探しより理解を深めること。

name: 社会科学特講（災害と公害の社会学）
summary_excerpt: 本講義では、私たちの社会が直面する重要な課題である「災害」と「公害」を、......
subject_name: 社会科学特講（災害と公害の社会学）
subject_category: 先進専門科目・先進社会科学特殊講義
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
summary: 本講義では、私たちの社会が直面する重要な課題である「災害」と「公害」を、単なる自然現象や技術的問題としてではなく、社会的な事象として捉え、そのメカニズムと影響、そして対応と再編のあり方について社会学的に探求します。探究の対象は、洪水や旱魃のような自然現象に起因するものから、生物種や有害物質に起因する事態、そして武力攻撃や産業事故といった人為的事象まで多岐にわたります。火山噴煙の降下や不発弾の残存、放射性物質の沈着のように、その影響が長期化・広域化し、心と身体を傷つける公害に発展するような複合災害にも着目します。講義を通じて、これらの過程を社会学の視座から分析するための理論的・実証的基礎を身につけます。とくに、特定の社会構造が災害リスクを増幅させる仕組み、発災後は社会の混乱と不確実性が高まり、住まいや稼ぎを不安定化させ、暮らしぶりが変わっていく過程、そして長年をかけた地域の復興と生活再建にみられる社会的帰結、という一連の力学を学びます。国内外の事例、とくに原子力災害の場合を通じ、現代社会における災害の多角的理解と、その課題に対する批判的考察力、そして実践的な取り組みの着想を涵養します。
goal:
(1)災害社会学の理論的発展について歴史的経緯を含めて述べることができる
(2)リスク論、ハザード論、文化論、移動論などの理論的枠組みを用いて災害事例を分析することができる
(3)平成期の主要な災害事例について、社会学的観点から多角的に考察することができる
(4)災害社会学の研究方法を理解し、独自の調査・分析計画を立案することができる
study_outside:
事前学習： 各回の授業前に、Waseda Moodleに掲載する指定文献やレジュメの事前読了を求める。また、関連する新聞記事やオンライン資料の収集・整理を行う。各回の事前学習には90分〜120分を要すると想定される。
事後学習： 授業内容に関する振り返りレポート（400字程度）の作成、関連文献の追加読了、次回授業に向けた論点整理を行う。また、独立リサーチ課題として、各回のテーマに関連する調査・分析レポートの作成を求める。各回の事後学習には90分〜120分を要すると想定される。

grading_method:
◆平常点（30%）：授業前の事前課題(10%)、授業内のワーク・討論に取り組み（10％）、振り返りを提出する(10%)。批判的思考と協調的作業の程度を評価する。
◆確認テスト（30%）：授業の中で筆記試験を実施し、事実関係の理解度、受講生自身の興味・関心内容、そして学期末レポートに向けた準備の状況を評価する。
◆学期末レポート（40%）：授業のうち１回分のテーマを取り上げ、国内外の災害事例をひとつ選び、社会調査（文献読解orデータ分析）を行い、被災と再生の過程について考察する（約9000字）。教場試験にレポートの印刷版（２部）を持参し、口頭試問を受ける。
評価の基準は、[i]アカデミックな構成により問題、本論、考察を展開していること、[ii]災害過程および災害対応について事実関係を読み解き、自分なりの批判的な洞察を提示していること、[iii]『社会学評論スタイルガイド』にもとづいて説得的に引用と参照を行っていること。
notes_url:
課題へのフィードバック方法
振り返りについては、翌週の授業冒頭で全体に対するフィードバックを行う
小テストについては、実施当日に解説と講評を行う
最終レポートについては、Waseda Moodleの機能を通じて個別フィードバックを提供する
授業運営方法の工夫
各回において、学生への質問、グループワーク、ディスカッション、ディベートなどを実施する
災害体験者へのインタビュー映像の視聴、フィールドワークの実施を含む
学生による事例研究発表とピア・レビューを組み込む
履修上の注意：
災害体験者の心情に十分配慮し、センシティブな議論には注意を払うこと
独立リサーチでは一次資料の収集と批判的分析を重視すること
ディスカッションでは多様な立場・観点を尊重し、建設的な議論を心がけること
授業の魅力として、現代社会の重要課題である災害問題について、理論と実践を架橋した学際的アプローチで学べることが挙げられる
受講者に望むこと：
災害・公害問題に対する社会的関心と批判的思考力
文献読解力と論理的思考力の向上への積極的取り組み
フィールドワークや調査活動への積極的参加

name: ゼミナールII（メディア社会学研究／春学期）
summary_excerpt: 卒業論文を仕上げるために、本ゼミナールでは先端的なメディア論の文献を正確......
subject_name: ゼミナールII（メディア社会学研究／春学期）
level: 上級レベル
summary:
卒業論文を仕上げるために、本ゼミナールでは先端的なメディア論の文献を正確に読んで議論する力と、自らの関心に沿った社会問題を分析する力を養います。もちろん私たちの置かれたメディア環境は刻一刻と変化しますが、そうした変化に右往左往するのではなく、腰を据えた議論を深めましょう。
またゼミナール I と同様に、外部の人たちと交流する機会も作る予定です。具体的にはフィールドワークや夏合宿、そして他大学のゼミナールとの合同研究会が含まれます。こちらもゼミナール I と同様に、詳細は受講生と話し合って決めていきます。質的社会調査を実施する力、その成果を報告する力、そして一連の研究へと仕上げる力を身につけましょう。
goal:
メディア論の先端的な内容を理解する
質的社会調査の結果を報告できるようになる

name: メディアの社会学 １
summary_excerpt: メディア（media）とは、「中間」や「媒体」といった意味の「メディウム（med......
subject_name: メディアの社会学 １
subject_category: 基盤専門科目・社会科学総合分野
level: 中級レベル（発展・応用）
subtitle: ニュースの社会学を掴む
summary:
メディア（media）とは、「中間」や「媒体」といった意味の「メディウム（medium）」の複数形です。つまりメディア論とは、人と人や人とモノ、モノとモノの「中間」にある「媒体」への想像力を養う学問だといえます。この講義ではマス・メディアやソーシャルメディアといった、私たちが日常的に接している多様なメディアの媒介性に着目し、メディア論の射程の広さとメディア・コミュニケーションの奥深さを学んでいきます。
前半のパートでは、私たちを取り巻くメディア環境を歴史と現状という視点から考察します。日々の生活を取り囲むメディアやニュースのことを、常に意識している人は少ないかもしれません。まずはありふれた存在であるメディアに着目する意義と、その方法の基礎を学習しましょう。
前半で学んだ内容を踏まえて、後半のパートでは現代社会の諸問題をメディア論やニュース論の観点から考察します。マス・メディアやソーシャルメディアが日常生活に浸透した状況において、メディア論的想像力は社会問題を考える基礎になります。私たちの眼前にあるメディアは、どのような問題と結びつくのでしょうか。具体的な事例の考察を通じて、地に足のついたメディア論に取り組みましょう。
本講義では、受講生のメディア経験を具体的に話し合う機会を設けます。ぜひ議論に積極的に参加して、メディア論的想像力を主体的に身につけましょう。自分の言葉でメディアについて考えることを求めます。
goal:
①　メディア論の基礎的な用語や概念を理解し、自分の言葉で説明できる。
②　具体的な社会問題について、メディア論の知見を踏まえて分析できる。
study_outside: 授業中に読んできてほしい記事や論文を指定します。次の授業の内容の理解に資するものですので、積極的に読み進めてください。指定した文献の予習と授業で配布した資料の復習を組み合わせると、90分程度の事前・事後学習が求められます。
schedule_plan:
1:
第１回　オリエンテーション（本講義の目的と概要）：メディアとは何か
本講義の目的と概要について説明します。またメディアとは何かという基本的な理解を確認します。
2:
第２回　私たちを取り巻くメディア
新聞やテレビといった伝統的なニュースメディアから、インターネットやソーシャルメディアといった現代のデジタルメディアまで概観します。
3:
第３回　ニュース論の基礎
私たちの社会的な認識と現実の出来事を媒介するニュースについて、その特徴や国民国家との関係について学びます。
4:
第４回　ニュースはどのように生み出されるのか
ジャーナリズム論やニュースの社会学の知見を踏まえて、社会的な出来事がニュースになる過程を学習します。
5:
第５回　日本のメディア史①：新聞の誕生と発展
現在のメディア環境が成立した背景を理解するために、日本のメディア史について学習します。まずは19世紀末からの新聞の時代に着目します。
6:
第６回　日本のメディア史②：ラジオからテレビへ
1930年代から1960年代までのメディア史を学びます。第二次世界大戦（アジア・太平洋戦争）から占領、そして高度経済成長へという流れの中で、メディアが果たした役割を考えます。
7:
第７回　日本のメディア史③：テレビの時代の熱狂
1960年代から1980年代にかけて、テレビの時代が到来した意味を考えます。テレビは国民国家の統合に欠かせないメディアになった一方で、20世紀の革命を支えるメディアにもなりました。
8:
第８回　現代社会とメディア④：インターネットの衝撃と「情報化の夢」
20世紀末から21世紀の初頭にかけて登場した、インターネットの衝撃について学びます。とりわけ伝統的なマス・メディアと比較することで、インターネットに託された「夢」と現実を理解します。
9:
第９回　現代社会とメディア①：フェイクニュースの時代
現代のメディア環境を理解するうえで、フェイクニュースの氾濫を等閑視することはできません。この現状を批判的に考察する方法を検討します。
10:
第１０回　現代社会とメディア②：メディア化する政治
現代の政治や選挙は、メディアの論理と不可分な形で進行しています。テレビ政治からソーシャルメディア政治までの流れを辿り直すことで、政治のメディア化の現在地を確認します。
11:
第１１回　現代社会とメディア③：社会運動とメディア
社会問題を解決するための運動も、メディアとの関係を抜きにしては語れません。社会運動の多様性とメディアとの関係を理解し、ソーシャルメディア時代の社会運動の意義について考えます。
12:
第１２回　現代社会とメディア④：地方益と国益の対立
メディアに表出する「声」は、どういった立場を代弁しているのでしょうか。地方益と国益が対立する状況を眼差しながら、メディアと「声」の関係を探ります。
13:
第１３回　現代社会とメディア⑤：アテンションの時代のニュース
ソーシャルメディアや生成AIの発達によって、ユーザーのアテンションの奪い合いが顕在化しています。この状況をニュースの社会学の観点から考察します。
14:
第１４回　講義のまとめと教場試験
半期で学んできたことを確認したうえで、教場試験を実施します。しっかりと復習をしたうえで、試験に臨んでください。
textbook: 特定の教科書は使用しません。必要に応じて、資料を配付します。
reference:
講義の内容に関わるものとして、以下のテキストを推薦します。
大石裕, 2022, 『コミュニケーション研究』第5版, 慶應義塾大学出版会, ISBN: 9784766428100.
佐藤卓己, 2018, 『現代メディア史』新版, 岩波書店, ISBN: 9784000289207.
津田正太郎ら編, 2024, 『ソーシャルメディア時代の「大衆社会」論』ミネルヴァ書房, ISBN: 9784623097258.
grading_method:
割合
評価基準

60％

name: ソーシャル・リサーチ（災害と公害） I
summary_excerpt: 本科目では、災害や公害が地域社会や人々の暮らしにどのような影響を与える......
subject_name: ソーシャル・リサーチ（災害と公害） I
subject_category: 先進専門科目・社会科学総合分野（ソーシャル・リサーチ）
summary:
本科目では、災害や公害が地域社会や人々の暮らしにどのような影響を与えるのかをテーマに、社会調査の考え方と方法を学ぶ。雲仙岳の噴火および原発事故の事例を取り上げ、自然災害や環境汚染によって生じる被害や復興のあり方について考察する。授業を通じて、文献を読み深めながら実際に現地を訪れ、災害や公害を経験した人々や地域の方々と対話を重ねる。その中で、自ら調査のテーマを見いだし、資料収集と実地観察、インタビューと聞き書きを行い、調査の企画から実施、報告までの一連の流れを実践的に学ぶ。当科目は春学期に実施し、さらに夏季休業期間と秋学期期間にはもうひとつの科目もあわせて実施する。
■実習内容の履修にあたって
本科目では、科目運営上の理由から独自の登録方法および履修ルールを設けていますので、履修にあたっては必ず次のページを参照してください。
「ソーシャル・リサーチ」の登録方法および履修ルールについて

また、実習内容についてのガイダンス動画を公開していますので、次のページから視聴してください。

name: グローバルラーニング（東アジア史） Ａ
summary_excerpt: 東アジアの歴史を通して、現代社会にも通ずる普遍的な諸問題を考えていく授業......
subject_name: グローバルラーニング（東アジア史） Ａ
subject_category: 基盤専門科目・グローバルラーニング
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
summary:
東アジアの歴史を通して、現代社会にも通ずる普遍的な諸問題を考えていく授業です。歴史的な知識を獲得するだけでなく、教員が設定した問いに対して参加者同士が議論し、理解を深めていくことを目的とします。そのため、参加者は日本語と英語を用いて、積極的に議論に参加する必要があります。最終的に、一国史に留まらない大きな視点で歴史を見る姿勢を身につけることを目指します。
なお、参加者の数によって授業の形式を変更する可能性があります。また、参加者の関心・希望に基づいて、扱うテーマを多少調整する可能性もあります。
プレゼンテーションやレポートの課題を準備する際、生成AIが作成したものをそのまま提出することは禁止します。参考文献を利用する際には、必ず文献そのものにあたって原文を確認してください。剽窃および参考文献の捏造が発覚した際には、その時点で単位を認めません。
This course examines the history of East Asia in order to consider universal issues that remain relevant to contemporary society. The aim is not only to acquire historical knowledge, but also to deepen understanding through discussion among participants in response to questions posed by the instructor. Therefore, students are expected to actively participate in discussions using both Japanese and English. Ultimately, the course seeks to cultivate the ability to view history from a broad perspective that goes beyond the framework of a single nation’s history.
Please note that the format of the class may change depending on the number of participants. In addition, the topics covered may be adjusted slightly based on participants’ interests and requests.
When preparing presentations or written assignments, the submission of work generated entirely by generative AI is strictly prohibited. When using references, students must consult the original sources directly and verify the original text. If plagiarism or fabrication of references is discovered, course credit will not be granted at that point.
goal: ①東アジア史に関する基本的な知識を身につける
study_outside:
予習課題として、毎回日本語または英語の文章を読んでくることを課します。講義では討論の時間を設けるので、文章を読んでこないと討論に参加できなくなります。授業後には課題の提出を求めることもあります。予習には90分かかると想定されます。
For each class, students will be assigned readings in either Japanese or English as preparatory work. Since time for discussion will be provided during class, students who have not completed the readings will not be able to participate fully. After class, students may also be required to submit assignments. It is estimated that preparation will take 90 minutes.

name: グローバルラーニング（東アジア歴史認識） Ａ
summary_excerpt: 近代日本による戦争および植民地支配に対する歴史認識は、現在でもしばしば外......
subject_name: グローバルラーニング（東アジア歴史認識） Ａ
subject_category: 基盤専門科目・グローバルラーニング
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
summary:
近代日本による戦争および植民地支配に対する歴史認識は、現在でもしばしば外交上の問題となり、解決に至っていません。なぜ各国間で認識の相違が生じているのかという問題を、実際の歴史的事件を踏まえながら考えていきます。参加者には日本語と英語を用いながら、積極的に議論に参加してもらいます。以上の学習を通して、多元的に歴史を見る姿勢を身につけることを目指します。
なお、参加者の数によって授業の形式を変更する可能性があります。また、参加者の関心・希望に基づいて、扱うテーマを多少調整する可能性もあります。
プレゼンテーションやレポートの課題を準備する際、生成AIが作成したものをそのまま提出することは禁止します。参考文献を利用する際には、必ず文献そのものにあたって原文を確認してください。剽窃および参考文献の捏造が発覚した際には、その時点で単位を認めません。
Historical perceptions of modern Japan’s wars and colonial rule continue to generate diplomatic tensions and remain unresolved today. This course examines why differences in historical interpretation have emerged among countries, drawing on specific historical events as case studies. Participants are expected to actively engage in discussions using both Japanese and English. Through this learning process, the course aims to cultivate the ability to view history from multiple perspectives.
Please note that the format of the class may change depending on the number of participants. In addition, the topics covered may be adjusted slightly based on participants’ interests and requests.
When preparing presentations or written assignments, the submission of work generated entirely by generative AI is strictly prohibited. When using references, students must consult the original sources directly and verify the original text. If plagiarism or fabrication of references is discovered, course credit will not be granted at that point.

name: 社会科学特講（近代日本外交史） Ａ
summary_excerpt: 幕末から戦後に至るまでの日本外交を学びます。明治維新以降、日本は急速に近代......
subject_name: 社会科学特講（近代日本外交史） Ａ
subject_category: 先進専門科目・先進社会科学特殊講義
level: 中級レベル（発展・応用）
summary: 幕末から戦後に至るまでの日本外交を学びます。明治維新以降、日本は急速に近代化を進め、悲願であった「不平等条約」の改正を実現しますが、その一方でアジアへの侵略も進め、敗戦という形で破局に至ります。以上の過程は非常に複雑であり、異なる選択肢を取り得なかったのかは、現在でも検討に値する重要な問題です。歴史を学びながら、現代の国際関係を理解する視座を獲得することを目指します。
goal:
①近代日本外交に関する基本的な知識を身につける。
②歴史を多元的な視点で見る姿勢を身につける。
③外交政策や国際関係を議論するための考え方を身につける。
study_outside: 予習課題として、教員が指定した資料を事前に読んでくることを課す場合がある。また、講義終了後に授業に関連する課題の提出を求める場合がある。それぞれ90分かかると想定されます。
schedule_plan:
1:
第１回　ガイダンス
本講義の目的と概要について説明します。
2:
第２回　開国
幕末の日本外交について講義します。

name: グローバルラーニング（メディアとしての声） Ａ
summary_excerpt: 人間の声は、文字と並んで言語を伝える媒体であるだけでなく、個人を特定する情......
subject_name: グローバルラーニング（メディアとしての声） Ａ
subject_category: 基盤専門科目・グローバルラーニング
middle_field: 表現（美術／映像／演劇／音楽／芸能／文学／メディア論）
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
subtitle:
メディアとしての声
Voice as Medium
summary:
人間の声は、文字と並んで言語を伝える媒体であるだけでなく、個人を特定する情報や感情を伝える媒体でもある。本授業は、社会や文化における声を通じた情報伝達をとりあげ、メディアとしての声が、何を伝え、何を伝えないかについて、文字と比較しながら考える。コースの前半では、ヴォイス・スタディーズ（Voice Studies）の英語文献を読み、そこで学んだ理論や概念と関連する実践的活動（声の伝達実験等）を行う。コースの後半では、前半の学びを踏まえて各自がミニポスターを作成し、発表する。
The human voice, like writing, is a medium for conveying language, but it is also a medium that conveys information identifying individuals and expressing emotion. This course focuses on communication through voice in society and culture, considering, in comparison with writing, what voice as a medium can and cannot convey. In the first half of the course, students will read texts in English from Voice Studies and engage in practical activities that relate to the theories and concepts they learn, such as vocal communication experiments. In the second half, each student will create an academic poster based on their learning from the first half, and give a presentation in class.
goal:
本授業では次のことを達成できるようにする：
By the end of the course, students will be able to:
1)  メディアとしての音声について考える上で有用な理論や概念を理解する。
Understand key theories and concepts for thinking about voice as a medium.
2)  音声が何を伝え、何を伝えないかについて考えるための視座を獲得する。
Acquire perspectives for considering what voice can and cannot convey to others.
3)  上記の学びを踏まえた上で、学びの成果をミニポスターにまとめ、発表する。
Create an academic poster based on their learning and give a presentation.

Before class, read your assigned section and prepare a Japanese translation. From Week 9, prepare your presentation, including poster development.

name: グローバルラーニング（コミュニケーションにおける声と身体） Ａ
summary_excerpt: 他者の声を聴く経験は、聴覚の働きだけではなく、身体の複雑な関与によって成り......
subject_name: グローバルラーニング（コミュニケーションにおける声と身体） Ａ
subject_category: 基盤専門科目・グローバルラーニング
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
subtitle:
コミュニケーションにおける声と身体
Voice and the Body in Communication
summary:
他者の声を聴く経験は、聴覚の働きだけではなく、身体の複雑な関与によって成り立っている。本授業は、日常の音声コミュニケーションにおいて聴取者がどのように他者の声を受けとるのかについて、聴取と身体の関係に着目しながら考える。知覚と身体の関係を扱う現代の現象学を中心に、聴取経験における身体の役割をとらえる上で有用な理論や概念を学ぶ。コース前半では、声と身体に関する英語の基礎文献を講読し、そこで学んだ理論や概念と関連する実践的活動（声の聴取実験等）を行う。コースの後半では、前半の学びを踏まえて、各自がミニポスターを作成し、発表する。
The experience of listening to others’ voices depends not only on the sense of hearing but also on the complex involvement of the listener’s body. This course considers how listeners perceive others’ voices in everyday vocal communication with a focus on the relationship between listening and the body. Drawing on contemporary phenomenology that addresses the relation between perception and embodiment, students will learn useful theories and concepts to understand the role of the body in listening experience. In the first half of the course, students will read texts in English on voice and body, and engage in practical activities related to the theories and concepts they learn, such as voice perception experiments. In the second half, each student will create an academic poster based on their learning from the first half, and give a presentation in class.
goal:
本授業では次のことを達成できるようにする：
By the end of the course, students will be able to:
1)  声の聴取における身体の役割について考える上で有用な理論や概念を理解する。
Understand key theories and concepts for thinking about the role of the body in listening to voices.
2)  音声コミュニケーションにおける声と身体の関係について考えるための視座を獲得する。
Acquire perspectives for considering the relation between voice and body in oral communication.
3)  上記の学びを踏まえた上で、学びの成果をポスターにまとめ、発表する。
Create an academic poster based on their learning and give a presentation.

name: 社会科学特講（音声の美学） Ａ
summary_excerpt: 美学というと芸術作品や美しいものを対象とする学問ととらえられがちだが、私た......

subtitle: 音声の美学
summary:
美学というと芸術作品や美しいものを対象とする学問ととらえられがちだが、私たちが経験するあらゆるものがその探究の対象になりうる。本授業は、現代美学のなかで注目を集める「日常美学（
everyday aesthetics
）」の枠組みを拠りどころに、日常生活において音声を聴く経験を美的経験として考察する視座を獲得することをめざす。音声そのものが好みや心地よさの評価の対象となるとき、その価値判断には何がどう作用しているのか。授業内では、音声コミュニケーション、街の音声アナウンス、
SNS
の音声動画配信などの身近な音声場面を事例にあげながら、音声を美学的探究の対象とするために有用な概念や理論を学ぶ。
goal:
本授業では次のことを達成できるようにする：
1) 音声を美的経験としてとらえる上で有用な理論や概念を理解する。
2) 学んだ理論や概念を身近な音声経験に照らして考えることができる。
3) 美学における日常美学の位置づけを説明できる。

name: Japan and World War II: Historical Controversies
teacher: ホートン　ウィリアム　ブラッドリー
summary_excerpt: 【CJSP/TAISI Course】 During the last 75+ years, Japan has never entirely ......
subject_name: Japan and World War II: Historical Controversies
teacher_full: ホートン　ウィリアム　ブラッドリー
subject_category: Field Issues (Peace Building and International Cooperation)
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
summary:
【CJSP/TAISI Course】
During the last 75+ years, Japan has never entirely freed itself from the heritage of the Second World War. Irrespective of nationality and political leaning, the existence of Japanese aggression during the 1930s-1940s is well-known, although rarely accompanied by stable factual knowledge or critical contextualization. While some things are unknowable, we will try to expand the known; first outlining the history from the 1930s to the postwar period, and then examining some major issues. Of primary importance are negotiations about reparations in the 1950s, the comfort women issue, and the textbook controversy.
The primary focus will be Japan itself and southern areas, especially Southeast Asia. Ideally, we will have a field trip to the Yushukan museum in the Yasukuni Shrine, however this may be changed depending on the Covid-19 situation.
There may be other changes depending on the class make-up.
goal: By the end of this course, students should have a good basic knowledge of the history of the Japanese in southern areas and in Japan proper during World War II. They should also develop critical skills needed to expand upon and utilize this knowledge.
study_outside: Students are expected to read assigned readings before class and have materials available during class.
schedule_plan:
1:
第１回　Introduction
Introduction to the course, the readings, and to World War II in southern areas, particularly Southeast Asia. Introduction to the geography of the region.
2:
第２回　Japanese Colonialism
Japanese involvement in wider Asia did not start in 1942. This week looks at the early stages of Japanese expansion.
There will be an introductory lecture of approximately 20 minutes, group discussion of approximately 50 minutes, and a final full-class discussion and comments by the lecturer for 30 minutes.
3:
第３回　The War in Southeast Asia
Discussion of the war in Southeast Asia, most particularly the invasion of Southeast Asia and Pacific islands.
There will be an introductory lecture of approximately 20 minutes, group discussion of approximately 50 minutes, and a final full-class discussion and comments by the lecturer for 30 minutes.
4:
第４回　The Occupation of Southeast Asia
Most areas of Southeast Asia were occupied in some form or another. This week we will discuss the differences in various areas, and the Indonesian case in greater detail.
There will be an introductory lecture of approximately 20 minutes, group discussion of approximately 50 minutes, and a final full-class discussion and comments by the lecturer for 30 minutes.
5:
第５回　Representing History
Representing history is very important, and how some people in Japan do this is extremely important. This week we will have no regular class, but you must visit the Yushukan Museum and
write a 1-2 page report about what you see.
6:
第６回　The End of the War
Discussion of how the war in Asia came to an end..
There will be an introductory lecture of approximately 20 minutes, group discussion of approximately 50 minutes, and a final full-class discussion and comments by the lecturer for 30 minutes. This class will probably be online. TBA
7:
第７回　Mid-term test
Mid-term exam, covering geography and history of the war.
8:
第8回　Postwar Resolutions
In order to wrap up World War II, many issues needed to be addressed. This week focusses on Asia and the creation of a new status quo as well as Japan, its recovery, and the reintegration with the world, including the occupation, the San Francisco treaties, US military presence, Okinawa, etc.
There will be an introductory lecture of approximately 40 minutes, group discussion of approximately 40 minutes, and a final full-class discussion and comments by the lecturer for 20 minutes.
9:
第９回　Comfort Women I
Discussion of prostitution in Japan, comfort women in general. In order to deal with this subject carefully, we need to examine particular cases. This week we will try to focus on one country, and particular cases and contexts.
There will be an introductory lecture of approximately 20 minutes, group discussion of approximately 50 minutes, and a final full-class discussion and comments by the lecturer for 30 minutes.
10:
第１０回　Comfort Women II
Discussion of the debate over comfort women in its global and local contexts.
There will be an introductory lecture of approximately 20 minutes, group discussion of approximately 50 minutes, and a final full-class discussion and comments by the lecturer for 30 minutes.
11:
第１１回　Textbook Controversies I
One of the most contentious issues in recent years has been Japanese textbooks. Discussion of textbooks in Japan and in other countries in Asia and their coverage of World War II, as well as the educational, legal, and administrative contexts.
There will be an introductory lecture of approximately 24 minutes, group discussion of approximately 50 minutes, and a final full-class discussion and comments by the lecturer for 10 minutes.
12:
第１２回　Textbook Controversies II
Discussion of textbooks in Japan and in other countries in Asia and their coverage of World War II, as well as the educational, legal, and administrative contexts.
There will be an introductory lecture of approximately 20 minutes, group discussion of approximately 50 minutes, and a final full-class discussion and comments by the lecturer for 30 minutes.
13:
第１３回　War Responsibility
Discussion of the issue of war responsibility with respect to Japan and World War II, as well as to other wars and other countries.
There will be an introductory lecture of approximately 20 minutes, group discussion of approximately 50 minutes, and a final full-class discussion and comments by the lecturer for 30 minutes.
14:
第１４回　Exam and Wrap-up
Final exam (approximately 60 minutes).
Final discussion of the World War II and its lasting impacts. Has this war been treated differently than other wars? Should it be?

summary:
Who give us this day our daily breads?
The Lord's Prayer says, "Our Father in heaven" "Give us this day our daily breads." What about in this world? Who give us this day our daily breads, how, and why? We are going to study on dynamics of globalization of these providers in modern and postmodern times, particularly focusing on the making of Trans-Pacific foods between U.S. and Japan. Each student will join our collective projects of mapping the making process of the Trans-Pacific food provision through their own research about their favorite food-related ideas, products, and issues.
goal: The course aims to foster an understanding of economic growth in two cultures that vary greatly but have strong ties. Looking at how food and restaurants have developed on both sides of the ocean, students will re-examine their own economic understanding while learning about and comparing how foods and the businesses related to them have unfolded. Students will not just gain knowledge, they look at the foundations of this growth to examine how the US and Japan vary, learning to approach phenomena from different cultural starting points. Students will be encouraged to share their insight, based on their experiences and academic backgrounds, empowering them as educators and co-creating new ideas and knowledge.
study_outside: Before and after each class students are expected to work a lot for assigned task including reading, writing, researching and others and required to have completed all the things between classes.

summary:
【TAISI Course】
Manga and anime were considered to be an insignificant set of immature subcultures for a long time.  Today, they have turned mainstream as they discovered a wider and more serious audience globally.  Manga and anime are as both a window and a mirror to the Japanese society; an educated historical analysis should lead to a better understanding of the Japanese mind and the national identity.
【In-person Course】
This course is going to be taught in classroom only.
goal: This course is open to both international and Japanese students. For international students, one of the key objectives is to experience the wide variety of important works in manga and anime. For Japanese students, this course should provide a learning opportunity in intercultural communication and also a chance to rethink the contemporary Japanese identity. The classwork will also focus on the writing and presentation skills.

name: Women in Modern Japan: Contemporary Japanese Women
teacher: ホートン　ウィリアム　ブラッドリー
summary_excerpt: 【CJSP/TAISI Course】 During the postwar era, social and economic changes ......
subject_name: Women in Modern Japan: Contemporary Japanese Women
teacher_full: ホートン　ウィリアム　ブラッドリー
middle_field: 文化（比較文化・ジェンダー論・カルチュラルスタディーズ）
minor_field: カルチュラルスタディーズ
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
summary:
【CJSP/TAISI Course】
During the postwar era, social and economic changes have led to drastically changed contexts for
Japanese women. These changes could be characterized as a second wave of Japanese in which GHQ policy certainly played a role encouraging positive changes enjoyed by Japanese women. Prewar images like
“
good wife, wise mothers
”
and
“
modern girls
”
gave way to other images, including
“
education mothers
”
and the office lady (OL). Today both elderly and young women play important social and economic roles, despite occasional trivialization of their functions. We will examine images and issues related to women as well as relationships between men and women in Japan through academic works and newspaper articles. Students will also examine popular media for images of women.
goal: Students will obtain a good knowledge of some common “roles” which women are seen as filling, as well as a broader understanding of contemporary Japan. Students will be able to use this knowledge to explore popular media.
study_outside:
Students are expected to read assigned readings before class, and have materials available during class.
Other assignments should also be prepared before the class, as announced by the instructor.
Students should also take notes in class, and review them after class and before exams.
Discussion is critical for understanding, and so in addition to short lectures, every class will include substantial group discussion and a wrap-up joint discussion.

minor_field: 理論経済学・経済思想
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
summary:
【CJSP/TAISI Course】
This course aims to provide the basics of game theory. Game theory deals with situations in which an agent’s payoff depends on other agents’ actions. (For instance, a firm’s profit depends on a rival firm’s sales strategy.) We study how each agent behaves in such situations. The ideas of game theory are now applied not only to economics, but also to other research fields (such as political sciences, biology, computer sciences, and law). Knowledge of basic mathematics is required to understand the materials in this class.
goal: The goal of this class is to understand the basics of game theory. Students are expected to learn the mathematical structure behind the game theoretic situations.
study_outside: Basically, review quizzes or reading materials will be assigned once or twice in three weeks. They will take about 60 to 120 minutes to finish.

name: Disaster Studies
summary_excerpt: 【TAISI Course】 What did we learn from 3.11, the catastrophic tsunami and......
subject_name: Disaster Studies
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
subtitle: Minamata and Fukushima
summary:
【TAISI Course】
What did we learn from 3.11, the catastrophic tsunami and the nuclear meltdown in 2011?  The damage and loss were immense, and the Japanese people discovered that natural disasters are often closely intertwined with human failures.  To better understand 3.11 and Fukushima, experts agree that Minamata Disease is an important precedent.  A case of severe mercury poisoning from factory wastewater, Minamata Disease has affected tens of thousands of people since the 1950s, and the litigation continues even to this day.  Recovery from a disaster is slow as people suffer from prolonged physical and psychological trauma.
【In-person Course】
This course is going to be taught in classroom only.
goal: The main goal of the course is to consider what it means to be affected by a natural disaster, a human disaster, or a combination of both. Our primary textbook is “Rowing the Eternal Sea,” the first-person testimony of Ogata Masato, a fisherman-turned-activist. Ogata is noted for leaving the leadership position of a major activist group after a personal turmoil; the fascinating account of his life and experience provides a mirror to contemporary society. We will also study issues such as safety, compensation, and community building. Improving writing and presentation skills is another important objective of this course.
study_outside: For each class students are expected to spend 1 to 2 hours to read required texts and prepare for assignments.

name: Contemporary Social Affairs 1
teacher: ヤン　マニュエル　ミナミ
summary_excerpt: "War is hell," said General William Tecumseh Sherman. He knew exactly what......
subject_name: Contemporary Social Affairs 1
teacher_full: ヤン　マニュエル　ミナミ
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
subtitle: Genocide, War, and Imperialism
summary:
"War is hell," said General William Tecumseh Sherman. He knew exactly what he meant. After all, he was the man who led the “March to the Sea" during the American Civil War, mercilessly burning and destroying every Southern town he came across. The 20th century, sometimes called the "century of war," made his words far more prophetic than he could have ever imagined. At least 108 million people have in died in wars during the 20th century. This is a staggering number, in comparison to those who died from all wars through the entirety of preceding human history, between 150 million and one billion people. And even now, more than 20 years into the 21st century, wars show no sign of ceasing. Why have modernization and science, which supposedly contributed to human progress, not yet abolished this barbaric practice of "war"?
In 1947, three years after the term "genocide" was coined to describe the systematic destruction of particular race or ethnic group, comedian Charlie Chaplin remarked in his film Monsieur Verdoux:
"One murder makes a villain; millions, a hero. Numbers sanctify, my good fellow.”
What history and power make this absurdity possible? And what should be done to resolve it? In this class, we will explore the answers to these questions through various documentaries, texts, and vigorous discussion.
goal: For class participants to deepen their understanding of history and structure of power in contemporary society, relate their relevant experiences, and share their thoughts both in discussion and writing.
study_outside: Every two weeks, a reading/visual material will be assigned to you, along with a set of questions to which you will write a response.

4:
"Who the F*** is Columbus?"
Alamo, Native Americans, “doctrine of discovery”
5:
Silencing the Indigenous Past
anthropology, Eurocentrism, history from below
6:
"Killing at a Distance or...How I Thoroughly Enjoyed the Outing"
migration, trade, weaponry, Monroe Doctrine, Hiroshima/Nagasaki
7:
Guns, Germs, and Imperialism
pandemic, geography, technology, world history
8:
"The Bright Colors of Fascism"
slavery, racism, white nationalism, alt-right
9:
Fascism, Totalitarianism, and Democracy
propaganda, anticommunism, exceptionalism, holocaust

Vietnam War, radical trans-Pacific

subtitle: Reappraising Japanese society: past, present, and future
summary:
【CJSP/TAISI Course】
This course examines various aspects of contemporary Japanese society from historical and sociological perspectives. Apart from the widely shared view of Japan as an economic powerhouse and a society of high efficiency, Japanese society often looks something of an enigma, showing notable ambivalence between the homogeneous and the diverse, the traditional and the modern, and the Eastern and the Western. After a historical overview of the modernization process of Japanese society since the late 19th century, this course focuses on four sociological topics: social class, social welfare, family, and ethnicity. Exploring each topic and their interrelationships, the course attempts to acquire critical and analytical perspectives for understanding contemporary Japanese society as a whole. Each class proceeds with a lecture and pair/group discussions.

name: Introduction to Social Business Cooperation
summary_excerpt: This course introduces the field of social businesses, which aims not to......
subject_name: Introduction to Social Business Cooperation
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
summary: This course introduces the field of social businesses, which aims not to maximise profits as traditional businesses seek but to solve or mitigate social problems through new business models in international contexts. Social businesses with dual missions—social and economic benefits—require innovative ways to tackle social issues. In collaboration with various partners, international donors also have been promoting business activities as a tool for supporting development under international initiatives such as the Sustainable Development Goals. Looking at various approaches, which challenge social problems in our globalised world, this course considers the possibilities and limitations of business activities to realise a sustainable society.
study_outside: It is essential that students do the required preparatory reading for in-class discussions. Students are expected to carefully read and study the materials at home. It is strongly recommended that students take notes when they read them. Note-taking is not simply a means to record information, but it is also an important part of your learning. It is also useful for students to pay attention to media presentations of social problems and topics related to social businesses.

name: Theories of Social Business Cooperation
summary_excerpt: This course aims to develop students' knowledge of theories and ideas th......
subject_name: Theories of Social Business Cooperation
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
summary: This course aims to develop students' knowledge of theories and ideas that support the collaboration between international development and business activities. It explores important socio-economic backgrounds, which have fostered and advanced these theories and ideas, and key international initiatives, which have been accelerating multi-stakeholder collaboration. This course also introduces some practices, to which these theories and ideas are applied, and considers the extent that business approaches can contribute to improving the social problems concerned. This course helps students build a solid foundation of knowledge about what supports the current trend of social business and its relevant concepts that pursue a dual mission of social and economic benefits in our connected world.
goal:
Through this course, students will be able to:
- demonstrate an understanding of key ideas related to social businesses;
- demonstrate an ability to understand, investigate and discuss the underlying assumptions of different theoretical approaches and to compare them; and
- improve written and oral communication skills in order to present clear arguments based on both theoretical and empirical materials.
study_outside: It is essential that students do the required preparatory reading for in-class discussions. Students are expected to carefully read and study the materials at home. It is strongly recommended that students take notes when they read them. Note-taking is not simply a means to record information, but it is also an important part of your learning. It will also be useful in your future career.

subject_name: Social Business Cooperation Projects
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
summary: The international collaboration between business and development activities has grown and is being recognised in society. There are social entrepreneurs who desire to solve/mitigate social problems and change society in less developed countries. However, the causes of problems vary and require diverse, innovative ideas and flexible collaboration. This course offers an opportunity to develop a business idea for those who are interested in finding solutions to social problems through business approaches in their selected international contexts. This course focuses on understanding the process of developing ideas and making business plans while considering the needs and motives of diverse stakeholders. Students are expected to work collaboratively to improve each other's business ideas.
study_outside: It is essential that students do the required preparatory reading to contribute to in-class discussions and team exercises. Students are expected to carefully read and study the reading materials at home and actively participate in in-class activities. Students are also expected to take the initiative to work on their projects outside the classroom.

subtitle: Postwar Japan: from US Occupation to 21st Century Globalization
summary: This course will introduce students to major themes in modern Japan, focusing on modern Japanese history and social change. We will take an historical approach to Japan, but also examine the specific ways in which Japanese society has developed in response to both domestic and international changes. Such changes include the role of gender and women in Japan, the Occupation of Japan, the rise of national identity and Japan’s presence in the world, the emergence of alternative identities and consumption among youngsters, social movements, as well as neo-nationalism in 21st century Japan.
goal:
1. Increase your basic knowledge of major research on modern Japan.
2. Learn about major moments and transformations in contemporary Japan.
3. Improve your communication skills.

subtitle: Southeast Asia-Singular and Plural Palaces
summary:
【CJSP/TAISI Course】
Amid intensifying strategic competition between the United States and China, global supply chains have been shifting southward and Chinese investment in Southeast Asia has been growing. In this context, the 21st century may be regarded as a period of renewed economic and geopolitical significance for Southeast Asia. This course examines the historical and cultural backgrounds of Southeast Asian countries and explores both the commonalities and differences that shape the region.
In addition to providing foundational knowledge of the region’s history, culture, and political economy, the course will address contemporary challenges facing Southeast Asia, including economic integration, democratization, environmental issues, and the impact of major-power rivalry. Finally, as the regional order in East Asia becomes increasingly unstable, the course will consider how peace and stability might be envisioned and constructed in the wider region.
goal: ①　Students will be exposed to a wide range of knowledge of Southeast Asian countries as a complicated place, each with its own unique story.

name: International Human Resource Management
summary_excerpt: 【CJSP/TAISI Course】 This course introduces students to the fundamental c......
subject_name: International Human Resource Management
term_schedule: 春クォーター  01:水２時限／02:水３時限
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
subtitle: Introduction to international human resource management through case studies
summary:
【CJSP/TAISI Course】
This course introduces students to the fundamental concepts, theories, and practices of Human Resource Management (HRM) in a global context. As multinational corporations (MNCs) increasingly operate across national boundaries, managing human resources effectively has become a central strategic challenge. The course examines how differences in national culture, legal systems, labor markets, and institutional environments influence human resource management policies and practices.
Through lectures, group discussions, and case analyses, students will explore key HR functions—such as recruitment and selection, training and development, performance evaluation, compensation, and employee well-being—from an international perspective. Special attention is given to HRM issues faced by multinational firms, including expatriate management, cross-cultural collaboration, and global talent management.
*
This course will be offered in the srping
quarter
and will meet every Wednesday
during 2nd and 3rd periods for seven weeks.
goal:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:
1. Understand and explain core concepts of Human Resource Management and International Human Resource Management, including their strategic roles in organizations operating across national borders.
2. Analyze how national and institutional differences (e.g., culture, law, and labor systems) shape HRM practices in multinational corporations.
3. Identify key HR challenges faced by multinational firms, such as managing expatriates, diverse workforces, and cross-border teams, and evaluate possible managerial responses.
4. Apply theoretical frameworks and empirical insights from organizational behavior and HRM research to real-world international business cases.
5. Develop critical thinking and communication skills through group projects, class discussions, and written reflections on international HRM issues.
study_outside:
Before classes (60 minutes): students are required to read the materials posted on Moodle for each class (except for the initial class).
After classes (60 minutes): students are requested to review the materials for each class.

name: Development and Society
summary_excerpt: 【TAISI Course】 Contemporarydebates over whaling have largely been reduce......
subject_name: Development and Society
term_schedule: 春クォーター  01:火３時限／02:火４時限
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
subtitle: Environmental Consequences of Rising Demand for Edible Oils and Fats
summary:
【
TAISI Course
】
Contemporarydebates over whaling have largely been reduced to the question of whether theconsumption of whale meat is acceptable. Historically, however, whaling wasalso an industry devoted to the extraction of whale oil from blubber. Bydirecting attention to the production and consumption of whale oil, a commoditythat has now disappeared from modern life, this course seeks to reposition thehistory of whaling within the broader framework of global history and toconnect it to the environmental history of modern consumption.
In this course, wecritically examine the impact of modern lifestyles on the global environment,with particular attention to the production, distribution, and consumption of fatsand oils. The course focuses on the twentieth century and after, whenhydrogenation technology, which enabled liquid oils to be converted into solidfats, came into widespread use.
This technologybrought a variety of fats and oils to prominence as raw materials for margarineproduction, among which whale oil occupied a particular important place.Pelagic whaling in the Antarctic began in the 1904/05 season, and the oilobtained there came to be used in Europe as a raw material for margarine andhard soap. From that point until the collapse of the world whale-oil market inthe mid-1960s, whale oil remained a major raw material for margarine and soapin the European market.
Production ofsunflower oil and rapeseed oil, both severely disrupted by the Second WorldWar, recovered only in the early 1960s. From the 1960s onward, the productionof soybean oil, palm oil, and other vegetable oils also expanded rapidly. Thiscourse examines the decline of whale oil and the rapid rise of vegetable oilssuch as palm oil as interconnected historical processes, in order to deepen students’understanding of how these transformations have affected—and continue toaffect—the global environment, producing regions, and everyday life.
The course is organizedaround two principal components: (1) class discussions based on the lectures,and (2) group presentations. Students will work in groups to investigate the contemporaryproduction and consumption of the world’s four major vegetable oils—palm oil,soybean oil, rapeseed oil, and sunflower oil—and to analyze and present the effectsof these industries on the global environment and local communities. In doingso, students will also be encouraged to reflect on their own position as consumerswithin these global commodity chains.
study_outside: Students are expected to complete the assigned readings before class and to participate actively in class discussion. They are also required to conduct research outside class for their group presentations and final essay. In addition to reading assignments, students should prepare discussion questions or comments and review key concepts after each class meeting. Details of the group presentations - including group size, format, presentation length, and evaluation criteria - will be explained in class. Instructions for the final essay, including topic selection, format, citation style, and submission deadline, will also be provided in class.

subtitle: Why and How do companies cross borders?
summary:
【CJSP/TAISI Course】
Traditionally, foreign markets for companies were merely places where they exported domestically produced products and imported raw materials.  Today, it is common for companies to produce products abroad, provide services to local customers, and conduct research and development in many different countries, irrespective of their nationality, industry, or size.
Once the activities and organizations of a company become globalized across national borders, problems arise that cannot be fully explained in existing academic disciplines, which have assumed that companies remain within their borders. International business is an interdisciplinary/cross-disciplinary study using knowledge, concepts, and framework systematized in various academic fields, such as business administration, economics, politics, sociology, and psychology, to solve problems unique to international business.
In this lecture, we will study the principle of internationalization of firms. We will also consider what principle the internationalization of a company will increase efficiency and benefit the company, the individual, the economy, and society. Furthermore, we will learn how companies organize and manage various business activities that have spread around the world.
goal: The goal of this class is to acquire the ability to examine how changes in environmental factors surrounding a company will affect its internationalization. We will learn theoretically and empirically about how international business arises and behaves. By doing so, we will understand the dynamics of international business development, with a particular focus on Japanese firms expanding overseas and foreign firms entering the Japanese market. It also aims to understand how firms do/should organize and manage various activities that spread around the world, always keeping in mind the essential issues faced by cross-border businesses. This will help develop the ability to logically and empirically examine problems facing international business.

summary_excerpt: 【TAISI Course】 Regression analysis enables us to understand what is hap......
subject_name: Regression Analysis for Social Sciences 1: Basics of Regression Analysis, Analysis of Cross-Sectional Data
term_schedule: 春クォーター  01:水１時限／02:無フルOD
method_type: 【対面】ハイブリッド（対面回数半数以上）
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
summary:
【TAISI Course】
Regression analysis enables us to understand what is happening in reality through data. It also helps us to connect academic theories to the real world.
The emphasis is on understanding causal relationships. I discuss the relationships between regression analysis and artificial intelligence.
Regression analysis is based on a probabilistic idea and basic statistics. Knowledge of Statistics Literacy α, β, and γ is necessary.
This class covers regression analysis of cross-sectional data, corresponding to Chapters 1-9 of Wooldridge (2019).
Computer exercises using R accompany every regression method discussed in the class to apply them to real data.
This course consists of on-demand lectures and in-person lectures. The objectives of the in-person lectures are to provide a detailed explanation, Q&A sessions, and R exercises.
Please make sure to bring your laptop for R exercises.
Please attend the first in-person lecture after watching the first on-demand lecture.
goal: This course aims to promote an understanding of how regression analysis works and how to use it in practice.
study_outside: A pair of on-demand and in-person lectures covers one topic.
schedule_plan:
1:
Lecture 1　(on-demand) Introduction
What is the regression analysis? What is empirical research? Various types of data. Causal relationship and correlation. Basics of R. (Ch. 1 of Wooldridge)
2:
Lecture 2　(in-person) More on Introduction, Basics of the simple regression analysis
Detailed explanation of basic concepts, discussions, and R practice
3:
Lecture 3　(on-demand)　Simple regression analysis
The basic idea of simple regression analysis, ordinary least squares (OLS), Measures for goodness-of-fit, and the expected value and variance of OLS estimators. (Ch. 2 of Wooldridge)
4:
Lecture 4　(in-person)　A detailed explanation and discussion of the simple regression analysis
A detailed explanation and discussion of the simple regression analysis, R exercises.
5:
Lecture 5　(on-demand)　Multiple Regression: Estimation
The basic idea of multiple regression analysis,  OLS, the expected value and variance of OLS estimators, and the omitted variable problem. (Ch. 3 of Wooldridge)
6:
Lecture 6　(in-person)　A detailed explanation and discussion of Multiple Regression: Estimation
A detailed explanation and discussion of estimation of the multiple regression, R exercises.
7:
Lecture 7　(on-demand)　Multiple Regression: Inference
Statistica properties of OLS estimators, hypothesis testing, confidence intervals (Ch. 4 of Wooldridge)
8:
Lecture 8　(in-person)　A detailed explanation and discussion of Multiple Regression: Inference
A detailed explanation and discussion of inference of the multiple regression, R exercises.
9:
Lecture 9　(on-demand)　Asymptotics in regression analysis and Other Issues
Asymptotics in regression analysis, functional forms, specification, adjusted R-squares, prediction (Ch. 5 and 6 of Wooldridge)

summary:
How does economic growth occur? Why do such vast disparities in wealth exist between nations? This course explores these fundamental questions by examining both empirical and theoretical frameworks. We will investigate the underlying mechanisms that drive long-term prosperity and engage with the critical policy debates that shape the modern global economy.
This course utilizes a hybrid learning model to maximize both theoretical comprehension and practical application:
-
On-Demand Lectures
: Each week, students will watch recorded lecture videos to build their foundational understanding of the week’s theoretical or empirical concepts.
-
In-Person Sessions
: Seven out of the 14 scheduled classes will be held in person. These sessions are dedicated to exercises, group discussions, and interactive problem-solving based on the lecture material.
Note
: Students are expected to complete the on-demand video component before attending the corresponding in-person exercise or discussion class to ensure productive participation.

name: Building Local Peace: Field Work Practice
summary_excerpt: 【TAISI Course】This course provides an opportunity to ‘feel’ the reality ......
subject_name: Building Local Peace: Field Work Practice
term_schedule: 夏季集中  無その他
level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
subtitle: Active Discussions with Local Peacebuilders
summary:
【
TAISI Course
】
This course provides an opportunity to ‘feel’ the reality of peacebuilding.  We actively listen to the voices of various local actors in peacebuilding. After signing the national peace agreement, various actors worked to build local peace. Each actor has unique socio-political positions, visions, tactics, and partnerships locally and internationally. The uniqueness develops different achievements and challenges. Therefore, it is essential to learn about them to enhance your strategic and operational performance in a post-conflict society. This fieldwork in the Philippines will contribute to an understanding of the reality of building local peace through various local actors and their international partners.
IMPORTANT NOTE: The fieldwork is subject to cancellation or itinerary change due to the worsening of COVID-19 and/or security.
FYR, previously, I worked in the Atlantic Council in Washington DC (think tank), as secretary to a former Japanese parliament member (former Parliamentary Vice-Minister of Foreign Affairs), UNU hq., the Secretariat of the International Peace Cooperation hq. at the Cabinet Office of Japan, Japanese PKO missions in post-conflict countries, Hiroshima University, and JICA peacebuilding project implementation in the southern Philippines.
* Those students in the Field of Community and Social Development are also welcome!
* Those who have not met these requirements (see ‘preparation’ in the class plan) but have a solid passion for participating in this fieldwork may be subject to consultation.
goal: The goal of this class targets students to understand the work of local/international actors and their dynamism in peacebuilding in community, social, and institutional aspects. Empirically, students learn conflict sensitivity in peacebuilding.
study_outside: Please read the papers and project activities. All materials will be provided at the orientation. Before our departure for the Philippines, I will reach out to the students who register for this class and arrange our orientation to cover logistics and other necessary details.
schedule_plan:
Preparation: Before the fieldwork:
Students are required to study the distributed materials on international relations and the concepts and theories of peacebuilding beforehand.  For a deeper understanding, it is advisable to study the history of the conflict and their religion and customs.
Fieldwork and Practice:
We actively listen to the voices of various local actors in peacebuilding.  At the same time, we explore how the local actors engage with international actors and develop their partnerships into good/challenging practices. We learn these activities from the funders, such as JICA and/or the Minister of Foreign Affairs of Japan. In secessionist conflict, eagerness toward peace is different regionally. The ‘local’ can be defined differently within the Philippines. Therefore, we exchange views with those youth outside the conflict-affected areas.
The Bangsamaoro (Muslim Mindanao in the southern Philippines) peace processes have not been completed yet. Expectedly, we will listen to multiple perspectives on ‘Bangsamoro’ peacebuilding.  In this situation, we actively listen and discuss the respective manner of diverse values.
For your knowledge transformation, you will be asked to take time to document your rollercoaster day each evening.
On the last day of the fieldwork
We will hold a review session to digest your emotional experience and transform it into academic knowledge.
textbook: N/A
reference: N/A
grading_method: 30% Final Oral Presentation
notes_url:

The maximum is 8 students. In the case of more than 8 students, only 8 students will be assigned by a lottery.
The fieldwork is subject to cancellation or itinerary change due to the worsening of COVID-19 and security.
The expected fieldwork dates are July 27 to August 2 in the Philippines (Davao City and Davao Oriental).

Chronologically, this course explores peacebuilding support since the post-Cold War. We discuss the concepts of “Liberal Peacebuilding,” Post-Liberal (or Hybrid) Peacebuilding,” and “Everyday Peacebuilding.”
In class, we start with a digesting time (15 minutes), a lecture (60 minutes) and a discussion (25 minutes) to apply the knowledge to real-world peacebuilding.
Following the

Lastly, students will present their peacebuilding plan.

You are welcome to raise questions and issues in class. You are encouraged to engage and participate in class discussions for this course. We keep Chatham House rules in this class. level: 初級レベル（入門・導入）
summary:
This course provides students with an overviewof the field of Critical Development Studies. The course enables students tocritically examine mainstream development discourse and practice through avariety of lenses, including class and race. Throughout the course, studentsare encouraged to apply the theoretical framework of Critical DevelopmentStudies to actual development issues to enhance their understanding of andengagement with development practice.
Coursework includes group work exercises, classdiscussions, home assignments and presentations to develop students' criticalthinking and academic communication skills. Throughout the course, students arerequired to engage in reading, writing, and discussion. Each student willpresent a summary of an assigned book chapter, based on one-page summary notes.These presentations will be followed by group and class discussions.Additionally, all students are required to write a "reading journal"every week. In these journals, students will reflect on the week's readings anddiscussions, addressing elements such as unexpected revelations, confirmedassumptions, unanswered questions, or insights gained from external readingsbeyond the textbook chapters.
goal: The aim of the course is threefold: a) to provide students with an overview of key theories and approaches in Critical Development Studies, b) to encourage students to reflect critically on the challenges and opportunities of development practice, and c) to strengthen students' critical thinking and academic communication skills.
study_outside:
Pre-class Learning
Students are required to complete the assigned reading before each class. The readings are intended to provide a basis for discussion rather than a set of conclusions to be mastered. Students are encouraged to pay attention to how labour is described and interpreted in the text, and to identify questions, assumptions, or tensions that emerge from the reading.
Post-class Learning
Following each class, students should take time to reflect on the themes discussed and the questions raised. Instead of aiming for definitive answers, students are encouraged to consider the limits of their understanding and the perspectives they encountered. These reflections will support deeper engagement with the course and contribute to the final written assignment.

Students aregiven basic guidance on problem identification, design thinking, systemthinking and project design. Groups present their strategy and prototypeproduct in a pitch at the end of the course. In addition, the course encouragesstudents to critically reflect on how social problems are defined, whoseperspectives are prioritised, and what assumptions underlie proposed solutions.

summary: This course gives an overview of contemporary issues in gender and sexuality, with a focus on family, education, and work. Speciﬁcally, students will learn basic concepts, cultivate sociological  perspectives, and examine trends in marriage and having children, variations in families and partnerships, relationships among family members, the Koseki system, reproductive issues, education, and work, mostly in, but not limited to, Japanese society.

subtitle: Redesigning local communities and urban spaces by unravelling and connecting the bonds between people
summary:
Community design is a social and design movement that was first proposed in the USA around 1960 and has been practised locally for more than half a century since. The emergence of community design in the USA was against a backdrop of social changes triggered by earlier social backgrounds and urban problems. The methods of community design, which have emerged from practices in multiple specialisations such as architecture, landscape architecture, urban planning and environmental psychology, comprise a wide range of planning techniques and methods that transcend the boundaries of each specialisation. At a time when local communities are threatened with disintegration, can local communities and urban spaces be redesigned by restructuring the relationships between people and between people and between people and communities?
In this lecture, students will understand the history and methods of community design in Europe, the US and Japan, and learn about practical attempts in various regions to conceptualise the community design theory required by local communities in the second half of the 21st century.
goal: To understand the development of community design theory and practice, and to acquire the ability to think about the future demands of community design.

subtitle: The human mind to prevent or counter-violence
summary:
What causes violence, and what can be doneto counter it? This course discusses the human mind to prevent or counter-violence.
The course approaches psychological aspectsof peacebuilding with three themes: the causes and effects of violence, the causesand effects of nonviolence, and problem-solving. In class, we start with adigesting time (15 minutes), a lecture (60 minutes) and a discussion (25minutes) to apply theories and models to real-world peacebuilding.
Following the lectures, we apply theknowledge to conduct role-playing.
FYR, previously, I worked in the AtlanticCouncil in Washington DC (think tank), secretary to a former Japaneseparliament member (former Parliamentary Vice-Minister of Foreign Affairs), UNUhq., the Secretariat of the International Peace Cooperation hq. at the CabinetOffice of Japan, Japanese PKO missions in post-conflict countries, HiroshimaUniversity, Keio University and JICA peacebuilding-capacity development projectto implement in the southern Philippines.
goal: The goal of this course is for students to understand peacebuilding from psychological points of view.
study_outside: Reading a paper and/or an article will be assigned to each class. In addition, some practical research will be required before the role-playing.

subtitle: A Research Design Boot Camp for Advancement to Fieldwork: Developing Practical Field Research Competence
summary:
Course Overview
Enrollment in Global Lab 1 is recommended, though not required, as preparation for participation in the Global Lab field programs. Global Lab 2–4 are field-based programs conducted in three different regions. Students may enroll in one or multiple programs depending on their interests and availability. Students are also strongly encouraged to enroll in Global Lab 5: Synthesis, Analysis & Report in order to deepen their learning through post-fieldwork reflection, analysis, and reporting.
Course Description
This course serves as a preparatory and research design module for students seeking to advance to Global Lab 2–4 (Fieldwork Practicum). Its purpose is to cultivate the research attitude, methodological competence, and design capacity necessary for conducting field-based international research. The course is structured in several stages, including:
・On-demand lectures for foundational knowledge acquisition
・Intensive in-person workshops for critical review and refinement of research plans
・Field simulation exercises to practice observation and interaction skills
・A final research design review
Training will cover research ethics, regional contextual understanding, research methodology, formulation and refinement of research questions, research design, dialogue skills, observational techniques, and field responsiveness—integrated competencies essential for field research. In the final session, students will undergo an oral examination of their research proposal. This evaluation provides feedback on students’ readiness for participation in the Global Lab field courses. This is not a conventional lecture-based course. Rather, it is designed as a training-oriented program that emphasizes iterative cycles of preparation, practice, and reflection. Active participation, strong initiative, and responsible engagement are therefore expected from all students. The course aims to cultivate the practical competencies required for responsible and effective participation in field-based research.
goal: ・Independently construct a research design capable of supporting field investigation.

・Develop a draft research theme, hypotheses, and fieldwork plan.
・Prepare questions and comments for workshop sessions.
Post-Class Work:

・Improve field-response strategies and contingency plans.
・Finalize the research proposal for qualification review.

subtitle: Social Innovation in the Greater Bay Area
summary:
This course is a two-week intensive field program jointly conducted by Waseda University and TheUniversity of Hong Kong (HKU). Enrollment in Global Lab 1: Preparation & Research Design is requiredas a prerequisite. Students are also strongly encouraged to enroll in Global Lab 5: Synthesis, Analysis &Report in order to deepen their learning through post-fieldwork reflection, analysis, and reporting.Students will spend approximately one week each in Tokyo and Hong Kong, engaging in practicallearning focused on the theme of social innovation. Through workshops on identifying social challengesand developing possible solutions, as well as field visits to companies, local governments, and non-profitorganizations, students will explore a variety of approaches to addressing contemporary social issues.The course aims to develop international perspectives, analytical skills, and the ability to design andimplement practical solutions through collaborative learning in multicultural environments. By studyingin the two cities of Tokyo and Hong Kong—each representing different social and cultural contextswithin Asia—students will gain a deeper understanding of social challenges from comparative andinternational perspectives. Hong Kong, in particular, provides a unique setting where Eastern andWestern civilizations, institutions, and social values intersect. Through field-based learning in thiscontext, students will develop a first-hand understanding of the dynamics of intercivilizational dialogueand hybrid social systems in contemporary Asia. Through this joint program between the twouniversities, the course seeks to cultivate the practical capabilities necessary for students to becomefuture leaders of social transformation.
[Note]
This course requires a selection process based on a statement of purpose. Please be sure to submit your statement of purpose during the course registration period (approximately 100–200 words in English, or 300–400 characters in Japanese).
Please check the following link for instructions on how to submit the statement of purpose.

Students acquire the ability to understand social issues from comparative and international perspectives, and to collaborate with others in multicultural environments (International Perspective).
study_outside: Preparatory Study:
schedule_plan:
Course Plan
Japan Phase
Copyright © Waseda University 2006-2026.All rights reserved. Ver. 7.0.0
Learning in Japan begins with a workshop at Waseda University on July 27, designed and facilitated byWaseda students. The workshop introduces social innovation, design thinking, and Appreciative Inquiry,and focuses on relationship building and the identification of shared social issues.
From July 28 to July 30, students participate in a three-day field-based PBL workshop in a regionalsite(a multicultural community area in Tokyo or its surrounding region). Students conduct fieldobservations, interact with local stakeholders such as residents, local governments, companies, andNGOs, and identify key social challenges. Based on these insights, student teams develop prototypeideas and present them to local stakeholders.
On July 31, students return to Waseda University for a reflection and prototyping workshop. In thissession, students reflect on their learning in Japan and further develop ideas for addressing socialchallenges.
Hong Kong Phase
The Hong Kong phase follows the program of the “Global Bay Areas Impact Lab” coordinated byProfessor Olivia.
On August 2, students explore Hong Kong’s local culture and communities through guided urban walksand field observations.
On August 3, students participate in sessions at the University of Hong Kong to understand the socialinnovation ecosystem in Hong Kong. Activities include panel discussions with practitioners, collaborativeworkshops, and feedback sessions on emerging project ideas.
On August 4, students conduct field visits focusing on rural revitalization, sustainability, and multi-stakeholder collaboration, followed by group discussions and analysis.
On August 5, students integrate insights gained from both Tokyo and Hong Kong and participate inproject development and prototyping workshops.
On August 6, students present their final projects. Each team presents its social issue, key insightsfrom the fieldwork, and a proposed prototype. The program concludes with peer feedback and a finalreflection session.
Students return to Japan on August 7.

subtitle: Everyday Resilience in Highland Indigenous Communities
summary:
This course is an intensive international field laboratory conducted in Qingsquan Community, Wufeng Township, Hsinchu County, Taiwan. It explores community resilience in an era of complex and overlapping global risks. Students are expected to have completed Global Lab 1 and acquired fundamental social research skills prior to enrollment. Working in mixed teams with students from National Tsing Hua University, participants conduct field investigations, community dialogues, participatory observation, and problem analysis. The inquiry integrates multiple dimensions: disaster risks, geographical conditions, social structure, demographic change, everyday cultural practices of the Atayal people, historical memory represented by figures such as Zhang Xueliang and writer Sanmao, tourism dynamics, and local livelihood industries. Through these multilayered analyses, students reinterpret the community from the perspective of “Everyday Resilience,” focusing on how preparedness, mutual support, cultural continuity, and daily practices sustain long-term community viability. The final assignment is a structured field notebook that synthesizes observation, analysis, critical questions, and reflective hypotheses. This course must be taken together with Global Lab 3, in which collected data, insights, and research findings are further synthesized into a booklet to be returned to the local community. The course aims to cultivate interdisciplinary analytical capacity, intercultural collaboration skills, and the ability to generate context-sensitive knowledge grounded in real-world environments.
[Note]
This course requires a selection process based on a statement of purpose. Please be sure to submit your statement of purpose during the course registration period (approximately 100–200 words in English, or 300–400 characters in Japanese).
